executive_summary: The US dollar enters mid-June 2026 in the middle of a genuine regime change. The DXY trades around 100.6 (its highest since May 2025), having whipsawed from a sub-98 print on 15 June when the US-Iran ceasefire framework collapsed the geopolitical risk premium and unwound the haven bid, back above 100 within 48 hours as a newly hawkish Federal Reserve under Chair Kevin Warsh recast the 2026 rate path from cuts to a likely hike. Performance is two-sided: the broad trade-weighted dollar (Fed DTWEXBGS 119.5) is up about 1.4% over 1M and 1.8% over 1Y, yet USD is roughly flat versus EUR (1.1614), softer over 6M against high-beta AUD (0.7069), and firmer against JPY (160.41) and CAD (1.3994). The dominant macro narrative is an energy-led inflation shock: the 2026 Iran war and Strait of Hormuz closure pushed headline CPI to 4.2% YoY and lifted the June dot-plot median for end-2026 to 3.8%, implying roughly one 25bp hike rather than the cuts priced earlier this cycle. Valuation remains a structural headwind: the BIS real effective exchange rate sits near 107, in the upper third of its multi-decade range, and consensus bank forecasts set earlier in 2026 are broadly USD-bearish (EUR/USD 1.20-1.25 year-end). The central tension is the dollar smile pulling in two directions at once: de-escalation removes the left-side haven bid (USD-negative), while a hawkish Fed and resilient US exceptionalism reinforce the right side (USD-positive). Our headline stance is tactically Bullish with modest conviction on the hawkish Fed repricing, shifting to Neutral strategically as rich valuation, a synchronized global tightening cycle that compresses the dollar's relative carry advantage, and twin-deficit gravity reassert themselves over the 1-6 month horizon.

spot.convention: Rising DXY = USD strengthening vs G10 basket; falling = weakening

6m.direction: USD modestly stronger broad, mixed bilaterally

bis_reer.interpretation: US broad REER at 107.06 (April 2026) sits in roughly the 78th percentile of its post-2000 range, elevated after years of US exceptionalism and rate-differential support, leaving asymmetric downside if the relative advantage fades.

ppp_table.implied_fair_value: USD modestly rich vs EUR (EUR REER 103.97)
USD very rich vs JPY (JPY REER 65.7)

valuation.trade_weighted_assessment: The dollar is rich on a broad real trade-weighted basis (REER 107, 78th percentile) even after the 2026 sell-off, expensive against most of the complex but cheap against the strong-REER currencies (GBP, AUD, SGD).
valuation.what_is_priced_in: The market is paying up for the US rate and exceptionalism premium, leaving little valuation cushion. The REER signal is structural rather than a tactical trigger, functioning as a brake on dollar upside and a tail-wind for mean-reversion if the Fed-hike narrative stumbles.
valuation.actionability: Low on valuation alone; the REER acts as a strategic headwind to dollar upside rather than a tactical entry signal.

cftc.percentile_context: Aggregate net-long USD sits around the 60th-65th percentile of its 3-year range, meaningfully long but not at a crowded extreme.

positioning_summary.other_positioning_signals: Leveraged long is dominated by short-JPY (lev net -99,844) and short-CAD (-58,623), offset by long-AUD (+42,292) and long-GBP (+22,312).
positioning_summary.interpretation: Specs rebuilt dollar longs into the hawkish Fed and the JPY rate-differential trade, but the positioning is dispersed across pairs rather than a one-way DXY bet.
positioning_summary.contrarian_assessment: The most stretched sub-position is short-JPY/long-USD (USD/JPY near 160 and intervention-risk territory ~161), the most vulnerable to a violent squeeze on a BoJ hawkish surprise or MoF intervention; broad USD longs are not yet extreme enough to be a reliable contrarian sell signal.

market_structure_vol_summary.trend_regime: Counter-trend recovery within a 2026 downtrend; DXY basing 97-101, reclaiming the 100 handle on the hawkish Fed

moving_averages.alignment: Tangled, turning up; not yet stacked bullishly

volatility.rv_vs_iv: RV ~5.6% vs IV ~6.0% - small event premium, realised tracking just below implied
volatility.risk_reversal_25d_1m: -0.35 vols, EUR puts / USD calls bid - market paying a modest premium for dollar upside
volatility.vol_regime: Compressed/low vol with two-way event risk around Fed and Iran-deal durability

market_structure_vol_summary.key_observation: FX vol is compressed but with two-way event risk; the cheapest, cleanest dollar-strength expression sits in USD/JPY where carry, momentum and rate-differential align, though intervention risk caps it.

market_context.recent_regime: Price action over the past month has been dominated by two opposing forces resolving in the dollar's favour. The US-Iran ceasefire framework (announced 14 June, signed 17 June) collapsed Brent below $80 and unwound the geopolitical haven premium, briefly driving DXY sub-98 and lifting risk assets (VIX ~16). That dollar-negative impulse was overwhelmed within 48 hours by a hawkish 16-17 June FOMC under new Chair Warsh, whose dot plot removed 2026 cuts and signalled a possible hike, sending the 2-year yield to a 15-month high and the dollar firmer against all G10. The regime is best characterised as a synchronized global tightening shock triggered by energy inflation, in which the dollar's direction hinges on relative, not absolute, hawkishness.

peers.rate_path_next_6m: Hold; upside risk if energy persists
Own track points to 2.50% by Sep

key_spreads.interpretation: Wide UST-Bund spread underpins USD vs EUR
Huge yield gap drives USD/JPY toward 160+
UST roughly level with Gilt - neutral for GBP/USD
Near parity; AUD support is commodity/carry not yield

sovereign_yields.curve_shape: 2s10s +29bps - positive but flatter than a month ago as the front end repriced toward hikes

tactical.analysis: The dominant factor turned decisively dollar-supportive at the 16-17 June FOMC. The Fed held the target range at 3.50-3.75% but the June Summary of Economic Projections lifted the median end-2026 dot to 3.8% from 3.4% in March, an effective signal of roughly one 25bp hike rather than the cuts the market had carried into the meeting. New Chair Kevin Warsh removed forward guidance and delivered a curt, anti-inflation message, and the 2-year yield jumped to a 15-month high near 4.20% while the dollar firmed against every G10 currency. The US policy rate (midpoint 3.625%) ranks at the top of the G10 alongside the RBA (4.35%) and Norges Bank (4.25%), and well above the ECB (2.25%), BoJ (1.00%), CHF (0.00%) and SEK (1.75%). Crucially, the front-end repricing is what drives the tactical move: fed funds futures flipped to pricing roughly 66% odds of at least one hike in 2026, a stance no other major central bank fully shares. The UST-JGB 10Y spread near +274bps and UST-Bund near +179bps keep the USD/JPY and EUR/USD rate channels pointing dollar-up. The 2s10s at +29bps remains positive but flattened as the market priced Fed hawkishness into the front end. Against this, the dollar is already long and the hike is not a certainty; if July inflation cools on cheaper oil, the front end can give back gains quickly. On balance the rate channel is the strongest single tactical support for the dollar this month.
key_data_points: Fed funds target range 3.50-3.75%, midpoint 3.625%, held 2026-06-17
June dot-plot median end-2026: 3.8% (vs 3.4% March), implies ~1 hike
Market pricing ~66% odds of >=1 hike in 2026 (CME FedWatch)
USD policy rate ranks joint-top G10 ex-AUD/NOK
UST-Bund 10Y +179bps; UST-JGB 10Y +274bps
Next FOMC 2026-07-29
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has moved most of the way to pricing one hike (~66% odds), so the easy repricing is done; further dollar upside on rates needs a hike delivered or hawkish escalation. We see the front end as fairly priced-to-slightly-cheap for the dollar given sticky 3.3% core PCE, leaving a modest positive edge if July-September data confirm the Warsh inflation framing.

strategic.analysis: Over 1-6 months the relative-rate story is far less one-sided because the energy shock triggered a synchronized global tightening, not a US-only one. The ECB hiked to 2.25% in June (its first hike since 2022-23), the BoJ raised to 1.00% (highest since 1995), Norges Bank sits at 4.25% with a hawkish bias, and the RBNZ's own track points to 2.50% by September. As peers tighten, the dollar's carry advantage compresses even if the Fed hikes once: USD already earns negative carry versus AUD (-72bps), NOK (-62bps) and roughly flat versus GBP (-12bps), and its positive carry versus EUR (+138bps) and JPY (+262bps) is unlikely to widen materially if the ECB and BoJ are also moving up. The strategic question is whether the Fed out-hikes the field, and the evidence is mixed: the dot plot shows one hike, but so do several peer central banks' trajectories. If the Iran ceasefire holds and oil stays sub-$80, US headline inflation should decelerate from 4.2%, removing the rationale for further Fed tightening and reopening the 2027 cut narrative that bank consensus favours. The 10Y differential advantage (UST-Bund +179bps) is structural and supportive but already in the price, and has not prevented EUR/USD from holding 1.16. We therefore see the rate factor as broadly balanced strategically: dollar-supportive on level, dollar-neutral-to-negative on the change in relative carry. Confidence is moderate given genuine two-sided risk around the inflation path.
key_data_points: USD carry: +138bps vs EUR, +262bps vs JPY, +362bps vs CHF; but -72bps vs AUD, -62bps vs NOK, -12bps vs GBP
ECB hiked to 2.25% (11 June), BoJ to 1.00% (16 June) - synchronized tightening
Median dot implies ~1 hike to ~3.8% by end-2026
UST-Bund 10Y +179bps (structural USD support, already priced)
Consensus sees 2027 Fed cuts narrowing differentials
2s10s +29bps; flat-to-positive curve
strategic.what_is_priced: Consensus prices the dollar's rate advantage peaking in 2026 and eroding through 2027 as the Fed eventually cuts and peers catch up - broadly USD-bearish (EUR/USD 1.20-1.25). We are less bearish than consensus near-term but agree the relative-carry tailwind fades; the strategic edge is small and direction-dependent on the oil/inflation path.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical Bullish but strategic Neutral: the hawkish Fed repricing is an immediate, mechanical front-end driver (USD-positive now), but over 1-6 months synchronized peer tightening compresses the dollar's relative carry advantage and cheaper oil should slow US inflation, removing the hike rationale.

carry_vs_peers.interpretation: Attractive and momentum-aligned via USD/JPY, but capped by MoF intervention near 161
Positive carry vs the main reserve peer; modest
Highest carry-to-vol but CHF haven risk offsets
Negative carry - long-USD vs AUD relies on direction not carry

carry_analysis.carry_regime: Carry works best through USD/JPY where carry, the +274bps 10Y gap and trend all align; against AUD and NOK carry is negative so a long-USD expression relies on direction.
carry_analysis.breakeven_move: USD/JPY +262bps annualised ~ 22bps/month ~ ~0.35 yen of carry over 30 days; spot can fall only ~0.35 yen before a month of carry is erased - thin protection against a squeeze.

tactical.analysis: The inflation impulse is the proximate cause of the hawkish Fed and is dollar-supportive at the tactical horizon. Headline CPI accelerated to 4.2% YoY in May (released 10 June), the highest since April 2023, with a +0.5% MoM print driven overwhelmingly by energy (the energy index rose +3.9% MoM and accounted for over 60% of the all-items increase). Core CPI was firmer-than-comfortable at 2.9% YoY, and the Fed's preferred core PCE stands at 3.3% YoY (April, released 28 May), well above the 2% target. Supercore services ex-shelter rose to 3.7% YoY, signalling that the energy shock is beginning to pass through into stickier components. Growth is resilient: Q1 GDP was revised to +1.6% annualised but the Atlanta Fed GDPNow tracks Q2 near 2.8-3.0%, and US exceptionalism is widening as the same energy shock pushes the euro area into PMI contraction (48.5 composite) while US ISM Manufacturing (54.0) and Services (54.5) stay firmly expansionary. This combination of hot inflation plus resilient activity is the textbook backdrop for a hawkish central bank and a strong currency. The tactical risk is that the inflation is recognised as energy-driven and transitory once oil falls, which the trimmed-mean PCE at just 2.3% supports. For the next 2-4 weeks, though, the data flow argues the Fed stays hawkish and the dollar stays bid.
key_data_points: Headline CPI 4.2% YoY, +0.5% MoM (May, released 2026-06-10)
Core CPI 2.9% YoY; core PCE 3.3% YoY (April)
Supercore (services ex-shelter) 3.7% YoY
Trimmed-mean PCE only 2.3% YoY - energy-concentrated
Atlanta Fed GDPNow Q2 ~2.8-3.0%; Q1 GDP +1.6%
ISM Manufacturing 54.0, ISM Services 54.5 (May)
US vs euro-area composite PMI: 51.7 vs 48.5
tactical.what_is_priced: The hot CPI is now in the price - the Fed dot plot and the 2Y yield already reflect it. The edge is in the persistence question: the market is split on whether 4.2% headline is a transient energy spike or the start of broader re-acceleration. We lean transient (trimmed-mean 2.3%), which is dollar-negative strategically but not yet tactically.

strategic.analysis: Strategically the inflation-growth mix is more ambiguous for the dollar. If the Iran ceasefire holds and Brent stays sub-$80, the energy-led spike in headline CPI should fade meaningfully over the next two to three prints, dragging headline back toward the high-2s/low-3s and removing the principal justification for further Fed hikes. Core PCE at 3.3% is sticky but the breadth measures (trimmed-mean 2.3%) suggest the underlying trend is far cooler than the headline, which over a 1-6 month horizon argues for disinflation rather than entrenchment. US growth exceptionalism is the more durable dollar support: Q2 GDPNow near 3% versus euro-area contraction, ISM surveys in expansion, and a labour market still adding jobs (NFP +172k in May, unemployment 4.3%) give the US a relative-growth edge that typically supports the currency regardless of the inflation path. But the consumer is wobbling: University of Michigan sentiment hit a record low before a small June bounce, and the cost-of-living squeeze from energy threatens H2 demand. The net is a balanced strategic picture: disinflation undercuts the rate-hike dollar story while exceptionalism cushions the downside. We hold Neutral with moderate confidence.
key_data_points: Core PCE 3.3% YoY vs trimmed-mean 2.3% - wide gap implies energy-concentration
NFP +172k (May), unemployment 4.3%, AHE +3.4% YoY
JOLTS openings 7.62m (April, beat)
UMich sentiment record low ~44.8 (May), ~48.9 early June
Euro-area Q1 GDP +0.1% QoQ vs US +1.6% annualised
Cheaper oil (Brent sub-$80) disinflationary into H2
strategic.what_is_priced: Consensus expects US inflation to roll over as energy normalises and the Fed to resume cuts in 2027, with growth slowing modestly - broadly dollar-bearish strategically. We are roughly aligned: the disinflation case is credible, so the strategic edge from this factor is limited and we see it as a wash rather than a directional driver.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical Bullish (hot CPI feeds an immediate hawkish Fed) versus strategic Neutral: cheaper post-ceasefire oil should drive headline inflation lower over coming months, undercutting the hike rationale, while resilient growth offsets the disinflation dollar-drag.

tactical.analysis: The risk channel is currently dollar-negative through the left side of the dollar smile. The US-Iran ceasefire framework collapsed the geopolitical risk premium, pulling VIX down to ~16.2 (from ~22 in early June) and lifting equities, which mechanically unwinds the safe-haven dollar demand that had accumulated during the spring conflict. The dollar's first reaction to the deal was a sharp drop to a 10-day low and a sub-98 DXY print, the classic de-escalation haven-unwind. With the VIX in the mid-teens and credit spreads tight, the global backdrop is risk-on, which historically pulls the dollar toward the weak bottom of the smile as carry flows leave the dollar for higher-beta currencies (AUD up 9% over 1Y is the expression). The tactical complication is that the hawkish Fed simultaneously reasserted the right side of the smile (US exceptionalism), so the net risk impulse is dollar-negative but partially offset. For the next few weeks, with the ceasefire holding and risk appetite firm, the haven bid stays drained and the risk factor leans against the dollar. The convexity matters: the ceasefire is a 60-day framework, not a settled peace, so a breakdown would snap the haven bid back on. On balance the immediate impulse is dollar-soft with an under-priced upside tail.
key_data_points: VIX 16.2 (15 June), down from ~22 in early June
DXY hit 10-day low / sub-98 on the ceasefire (15 June)
Risk-on equity rally; S&P near record territory
Brent sub-$80 removes the energy/geopolitical premium
AUD/USD +9.1% 1Y - high-beta outperformance signals risk-on
Credit spreads tight (risk-on regime)
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has priced the de-escalation and the haven unwind - DXY already gapped lower then recovered on the Fed, not on renewed risk-off. The edge is in ceasefire fragility: the deal is a 60-day framework, so a breakdown would snap the haven bid back on and reprice the dollar higher, a tail the risk-on market is under-pricing.

strategic.analysis: Over 1-6 months the risk picture is genuinely two-sided, which is the essence of the dollar smile. On the bottom-of-smile side, a durable Iran ceasefire plus synchronized global growth would keep risk appetite firm and the dollar soft as capital rotates into higher-beta and EM currencies. On the left side, the ceasefire is fragile (nuclear terms reportedly disputed, a 60-day clock), US midterms in November add political risk, and any global growth scare or equity correction would restore the dollar's haven premium quickly. The dollar's haven status is structurally intact even if cyclically dormant: in any acute risk-off the dollar remains the first port of call. Because the strategic environment could plausibly sit at either the weak bottom or the strong left of the smile depending on the ceasefire and the global cycle, the risk factor is best scored Neutral. The balance of probabilities slightly favours continued risk-on (dollar-soft), but the convexity of the haven payoff in a tail event keeps us from a directional Bearish. Equity valuations are elevated, so a correction is a live haven catalyst.
key_data_points: VIX ~16 - low/complacent, room to spike
Iran ceasefire a 60-day framework, not settled - fragile
US midterms 2026-11-03 add H2 political risk
Dollar smile: haven bid dormant but structurally intact
Risk-on rotation favours AUD/EM over USD
Equity valuations elevated -> correction risk a haven tail
strategic.what_is_priced: Consensus prices a benign risk environment and a soft dollar as the base case. We agree on the central path but flag that the cheap VIX under-prices the convex haven payoff if the ceasefire fails or equities wobble - making strategic risk a Neutral with an asymmetric upside tail for the dollar.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both horizons lean against a strong dollar, but tactical is Bearish (the fresh de-escalation is actively draining the haven bid now) while strategic is Neutral (the ceasefire's fragility and the dollar's intact haven convexity balance the risk-on rotation over 1-6 months).

tactical.analysis: Aggregate speculative positioning is moderately long dollars but not at a contrarian extreme. Summing the inverse of G10 net positions on the latest CFTC report (week of 2026-06-09), leveraged funds are net ~+122,000 contracts long USD and asset managers ~+28,500 long. The composition matters more than the headline: the leveraged long is dominated by short-JPY (lev net -99,844 contracts) and short-CAD (-58,623), partly offset by long-AUD (+42,292) and long-GBP (+22,312). So the long dollar is really a short-JPY/short-CAD trade plus assorted offsets, not a uniform DXY bet. Net aggregate USD length sits around the 60th-65th percentile of its 3-year range, long enough to provide some unwind risk but not crowded. Open interest in EUR (871k) and JPY (505k) is heavy, reflecting deep engagement. Tactically this is a Neutral signal: positioning neither confirms a strong dollar trend nor warns of an imminent reversal, though the concentrated short-JPY leg is the vulnerable pocket. Weekly flow showed specs rebuilding dollar longs into the Fed, consistent with momentum rather than exhaustion.

Asset managers net ~+28,451 long USD
Short-JPY leg: lev net -99,844; short-CAD -58,623
Long-AUD +42,292; long-GBP +22,312 (offsets)
Aggregate USD length ~60th-65th percentile (3Y)
EUR open interest 871,507; JPY 505,075
tactical.what_is_priced: Positioning shows the market already leaning long dollars into the hawkish Fed, so the bullish flow catalyst is partly spent. The edge is recognising that the length is concentrated in short-JPY - a crowded sub-trade vulnerable to a BoJ/MoF squeeze - rather than a broad dollar bet, so a JPY squeeze need not mean a broad dollar reversal.

strategic.analysis: Over the strategic horizon, positioning is a mild headwind to further dollar gains but not a decisive driver. The aggregate net-long-USD at the ~60th-65th percentile leaves room to extend if the Fed actually hikes, but also leaves the dollar exposed to a position-unwind if the inflation spike fades and the 2027-cut narrative reasserts. Reserve-manager flows remain a slow structural negative: IMF COFER data show the USD share of global reserves gradually declining (~58% from ~70% in 2000), a multi-year diversification trend that the 2026 Moody's Aa1 rating and twin-deficit backdrop reinforce. Petrodollar accumulation, a marginal dollar support, weakens with oil sub-$80. The strategic positioning signal is therefore genuinely balanced: cyclical specs are long enough to unwind, structural reserve flows lean against the dollar, and neither is at an actionable extreme. We hold Neutral with the contrarian risk skewed slightly toward a dollar-long unwind if the hawkish-Fed thesis disappoints.
key_data_points: Aggregate USD length ~60th-65th percentile - room to unwind
USD reserve share ~58%, structural decline
Petrodollar support fades with Brent sub-$80
Short-JPY the most crowded leg (lev -99,844)
Asset-manager length modest (+28k) - real money not extreme
Moody's Aa1 / twin deficit reinforce diversification
strategic.what_is_priced: The market is positioned for the hawkish-Fed dollar to persist; reserve diversification is a known slow-burn that markets largely shrug off. The edge is that consensus under-weights how a single soft CPI could trigger a disproportionate unwind of the ~60th-percentile dollar length, especially the short-JPY leg.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both horizons are Neutral; confidence is marginally higher tactically because the latest CFTC snapshot is concrete, whereas the strategic read layers in slow-moving reserve flows that are directionally bearish but rarely act as a near-term trigger.
factors.contrarian_analysis_tactical: Positioning is moderately long dollars (~60th-65th percentile) but not stretched at the aggregate level; stretched for the broad dollar would require the low-90s percentile, which only the short-JPY sub-position approaches. The most likely unwind trigger is a soft US CPI/PCE print or a BoJ hawkish surprise/MoF intervention near USD/JPY 161, either of which would force short-JPY covering. A squeeze would be violent in USD/JPY specifically (lev net -99,844 is a large one-way book) but more muted in broad DXY terms because longs are dispersed. The other side of the trade sees the dollar's rate advantage as already peaked and is positioning for the 2027-cut, energy-disinflation reversal.
factors.contrarian_analysis_strategic: Strategically the contrarian case is that the entire hawkish-Fed, strong-dollar trade is a late-cycle crowding into a currency that is rich on REER (107) and structurally undermined by reserve diversification and twin deficits. If oil-driven inflation fades and the Fed reverts to the consensus 2027-cut path, the ~60th-percentile dollar length unwinds into a market already forecasting EUR/USD 1.20-1.25, amplifying the move. The squeeze risk is asymmetric: dollar longs are betting on a regime that depends on energy inflation persisting, which the ceasefire directly undermines.

tactical.analysis: The external balance is a slow-moving structural factor with little tactical signal, so it scores Neutral over 2-4 weeks. The US current account deficit was 3.6% of GDP for full-year 2025 ($1.12tn), though it narrowed sharply in Q4 to 2.4% of GDP as tariffs compressed imports more than exports. The US is now a net energy exporter, so the collapse in oil prices post-ceasefire is a marginal terms-of-trade negative (lower energy export values) but a consumer/inflation positive - a wash for the dollar tactically. Trade-policy noise (tariffs, the Treasury FX report) is the more acute near-term channel, but no fresh escalation is dated within the tactical window. The exorbitant privilege of reserve-currency status means the deficit is financed cheaply and rarely moves the dollar over weeks. With no dated external-balance catalyst and offsetting energy effects, the factor is genuinely directionless tactically; it would only flare if a tariff announcement or a weak Treasury auction hit within the window. The USD invoicing share (~40-50% of global trade) provides a structural demand floor regardless of the balance.
key_data_points: Current account -3.6% GDP (2025); -2.4% GDP Q4 (tariff-driven narrowing)
Goods deficit $241.5bn Q4; services surplus $81.4bn
US net energy exporter - oil sub-$80 a mild ToT negative
Reserve-currency status finances deficit cheaply
No dated tariff/auction catalyst in tactical window
USD invoicing share ~40-50% - structural demand floor
tactical.what_is_priced: The market treats the US external deficit as a permanent, well-financed feature and prices essentially no tactical risk from it. We agree there is no near-term edge here; the factor only becomes tradeable around a specific trade-policy shock, which is not currently scheduled.

strategic.analysis: At the strategic weight (0.16, the second-highest), the external balance is a meaningful dollar headwind. The twin deficit - a current-account gap of ~3.6% of GDP plus a federal deficit near 5.8% of GDP - is a structural drag that, while chronically financed, contributes to the multi-year erosion of the dollar's real value and the gradual diversification away from USD reserves (~58% share, down from ~70%). The 2026 dollar weakness to a four-year low earlier in the year was partly this structural story reasserting itself after years of exceptionalism-driven richness. Cheaper oil cuts both ways: it modestly trims US energy-export receipts but also narrows the goods deficit and supports the consumer. The terms-of-trade impulse from the energy collapse is net mildly negative for the dollar versus petro-currencies (the dollar's mild oil-positive correlation works in reverse as oil falls), though the effect is small. Over 1-6 months, with valuation rich and the rate-advantage tailwind fading, the external balance is one of the factors that tilts the strategic dollar picture toward softness. Confidence is moderate because the deficit's chronic financeability blunts its timing.
key_data_points: Twin deficit: CA ~-3.6% GDP + fiscal ~-5.8% GDP
USD reserve share ~58%, structural decline
2026 DXY fell to 4-year low ~97 on structural story
Oil sub-$80 trims US energy export receipts (mild ToT drag)
NIIP deeply negative (~-$20tn) but dark-matter income positive
REER 107 (78th pct) - rich, mean-reversion pressure
strategic.what_is_priced: Consensus explicitly prices the twin-deficit and reserve-diversification story as a core reason for its USD-bearish 2026-27 forecasts (EUR/USD 1.20-1.25). We are aligned with this strategic bearishness, so the factor reinforces rather than diverges from consensus; the edge is in timing it against the offsetting near-term rate support.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical Neutral (no dated external catalyst; offsetting energy effects) but strategic Bearish (the twin deficit and reserve diversification carry the second-highest strategic weight and reassert as the rate tailwind fades over 1-6 months).

tactical.analysis: Fed communication turned sharply hawkish and is dollar-supportive tactically. The June statement was notably terse, removed prior easing-bias and forward-guidance language, and new Chair Warsh explicitly declined to provide forward guidance while stating the Committee will deliver price stability in a strong, unanimous and unambiguous message. The dot plot is the highest-signal element: lifting the median end-2026 dot to 3.8% from 3.4% is an unambiguous hawkish guidance shift, and the 12-0 vote underlines unity. Markets read it correctly, repricing toward a hike and bidding the dollar against all G10. The transition to a Trump-appointed chair initially raised Fed-independence concerns (dollar-negative), but Warsh's overtly anti-inflation debut actually reassured markets that the new Fed will not be politically dovish - a credibility positive in the near term. For the next few weeks the guidance vector points clearly dollar-up: the Fed has told the market it is more likely to hike than cut, and the data are not yet contradicting it. The next checkpoints are the July minutes and Fedspeak, where any softening would matter.
key_data_points: June statement removed forward guidance; will deliver price stability
Dot median end-2026 raised to 3.8% (from 3.4%)
12-0 unanimous hold vote - unity signal
New Chair Warsh sworn in 22 May 2026 (Trump-appointed)
Market repriced to ~66% odds of a 2026 hike post-meeting
Next Fedspeak/minutes: July (minutes ~3 weeks post-meeting)
tactical.what_is_priced: The hawkish guidance is in the price - the dollar and the 2Y already moved. The edge lies in credibility durability: the market is treating Warsh's hawkish debut at face value, but if he is later perceived as bending to political pressure to cut, the credibility premium reverses sharply. Tactically that risk is dormant.

strategic.analysis: Strategically, guidance and credibility are a genuine two-sided risk and the most distinctive feature of the 2026 dollar setup. On one hand, a hawkish, independent-looking Fed that follows through on its inflation mandate is credibility-positive and dollar-supportive. On the other, the elephant in the room is Fed-independence risk: Warsh was nominated by President Trump after public attacks on Powell for not cutting, and the institution is undergoing a leadership-driven overhaul amid ongoing political scrutiny. If, over the next 1-6 months, the new Fed is seen to ease for political rather than data reasons, or if the administration escalates pressure, the dollar's credibility premium would erode, a structural negative that markets price heavily. The countervailing force is that Warsh's hawkish debut bought credibility insurance. Because the path could run either way - toward reinforced or eroded credibility - the strategic stance is Neutral, with the tail risk (politicised easing) being distinctly dollar-bearish and arguably under-priced. This is the factor most likely to surprise over the horizon. The September and December SEPs are the key guidance checkpoints.
key_data_points: Warsh Trump-appointed; Powell ousted as chair May 2026
Fed-independence a live 2026 market theme
Dot plot signals hikes - anti-inflation credibility (for now)
Strategic weight 0.12 - guidance matters more long-horizon
Politicised-easing tail = dollar-bearish, under-priced
Minutes (July) and SEP (September) next guidance checkpoints
strategic.what_is_priced: Consensus is split: some see the new Fed as a credibility risk (dollar-bearish), others as reassuringly hawkish. The market is currently pricing the reassuring read. Our edge is flagging that the independence tail is asymmetric and under-priced - a politicised cut later in 2026 would hit the dollar harder than a continuation of hawkishness would help it.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical Bullish (the fresh hawkish dot plot and Warsh's anti-inflation debut are an immediate dollar-positive guidance signal) versus strategic Neutral (over 1-6 months the Fed-independence tail - politicised easing under a Trump-appointed chair - is a material, asymmetric dollar-bearish risk).

tactical.analysis: The dominant event - the US-Iran ceasefire - has resolved in a risk-on, dollar-negative direction for the immediate window. The framework was announced 14 June and signed 17 June at Versailles, reopening the Strait of Hormuz, lifting the naval blockade and allowing Iran to resume oil exports; the result was collapsing oil, easing VIX and a drained dollar haven bid. With the most acute geopolitical risk premium removed, the tactical event vector points against the dollar. The offsetting risk is the ceasefire's fragility - it is a 60-day framework with reportedly disputed nuclear terms, so a breakdown within the window would snap the haven bid back on and reprice the dollar higher. The 60-day nuclear-negotiation clock runs to roughly mid-August, just outside the tactical window. For the next 2-4 weeks, with the ceasefire holding and no other binary US event dated, the event factor is mildly dollar-negative, with a fat tail if the deal cracks. Cheap event vol (VIX 16) makes hedging the tail inexpensive.
key_data_points: US-Iran ceasefire signed 2026-06-17, Versailles
Strait of Hormuz reopening; Iran oil exports resume
Brent sub-$80, VIX ~16 - risk premium removed
Ceasefire a 60-day framework; nuclear terms disputed
60-day clock to ~mid-August (outside tactical window)
No other dated binary US event in tactical window
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has priced the ceasefire as durable, embedding a calm dollar-soft outcome. The edge is the under-priced breakdown tail: a fragile 60-day framework with disputed nuclear terms could fail, and the dollar's haven repricing in that scenario would be sharp and largely unhedged at VIX 16.

strategic.analysis: Over 1-6 months the event calendar is dense and two-sided. The Iran 60-day window (to ~mid-August) is the first binary: a final deal cements risk-on (dollar-soft), a breakdown restores the haven bid (dollar-strong). The US midterm elections on 3 November 2026 inject H2 political risk - campaign-season fiscal and trade rhetoric, and the possibility of a government-funding cliff at the 1 October FY2027 lapse, are dollar-ambiguous-to-negative event risks. US-China trade friction remains a latent escalation risk that would be dollar-positive (risk-off/repatriation). Because the slate contains both dollar-positive (deal breakdown, China escalation, government shutdown disruption) and dollar-negative (durable peace, smooth politics) binaries, the strategic event factor nets to Neutral. The distribution is fat-tailed in both directions, which argues for cheap optionality rather than a directional spot stance. None of these binaries has a high enough probability to anchor a directional view.
key_data_points: Iran 60-day nuclear window to ~mid-August 2026
US midterms 2026-11-03 - H2 political risk
FY2027 government-funding cliff 2026-10-01
US-China trade friction a latent dollar-positive tail
Debt ceiling NOT binding until 2027
Event vol cheap (VIX 16) - optionality attractive
strategic.what_is_priced: Consensus prices a benign geopolitical path. We see the strategic event distribution as wider than priced in both directions - a durable peace is dollar-negative, but a ceasefire failure, a shutdown, or China escalation are dollar-positive tails the calm market under-weights, making this a Neutral with cheap convexity either way.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical Bearish (the just-signed ceasefire is actively dollar-negative now) versus strategic Neutral (over 1-6 months the calendar adds dollar-positive binaries - deal breakdown, shutdown, China - that balance the risk-on path).

tactical.analysis: Fiscal risk is a background factor with no tactical trigger dated in the window, so it scores Neutral. The federal deficit is running near 5.8% of GDP (FY2026, ~$1.9tn), debt held by the public is ~101% of GDP, and net interest expense has crossed ~$1tn (3.3% of GDP) - large numbers, but chronic and well-telegraphed. The debt ceiling is not binding (raised by $5tn to $41.1tn in the July 2025 reconciliation law) and is not expected to bind until 2027, removing the classic US fiscal tail from the near-term. Treasury issuance is heavy but auctions are being absorbed; there is no dated auction stress event in the window. The sovereign ratings (S&P AA+, Moody's Aa1 since May 2025, Fitch AA+) are stable with no 2026 actions scheduled. With no near-term catalyst, the factor is genuinely directionless over 2-4 weeks; it would only matter tactically if a specific weak auction or a surprise rating action materialised. The deep, liquid Treasury market continues to absorb supply without dislocation.
key_data_points: FY2026 deficit ~5.8% GDP (~$1.9tn)
Debt held by public ~101% GDP (gross ~$38tn)
Net interest ~$1.0tn (3.3% GDP) in 2026
Debt ceiling raised to $41.1tn; not binding until 2027
Ratings: S&P AA+, Moody's Aa1, Fitch AA+ (stable)
No dated auction/rating catalyst in tactical window
tactical.what_is_priced: The market prices US fiscal deterioration as a permanent, financeable feature and assigns essentially zero tactical risk to it. We concur - there is no near-term edge; the factor is dormant absent a specific auction or rating shock.

strategic.analysis: At the strategic weight (0.07), fiscal gravity is a slow-burn dollar negative. The combination of a ~5.8%-of-GDP deficit in a non-recession year, debt held by the public heading toward 120% of GDP over the next decade, and net interest doubling toward ~$2.1tn (4.6% of GDP) by 2036 is, on the CBO's own assessment, unsustainable. The May 2025 Moody's downgrade to Aa1 stripped the US of its last AAA, formalising the trajectory in the ratings. Over 1-6 months the more concrete channels are the FY2027 funding cliff (1 October) and the possibility the debt limit re-enters the conversation by late 2026 under the House budget plan, either of which would inject Treasury-market volatility. Heavy bill issuance also interacts with USD liquidity. None of this is an acute crisis, but at a 1-6 month horizon, layered on top of rich valuation and a fading rate tailwind, fiscal deterioration tilts the strategic dollar picture modestly bearish. Confidence is moderate given the chronic financeability that has defied fiscal-gravity calls for years.
key_data_points: Debt/GDP rising toward 120% by 2036 (CBO)
Net interest doubling to ~$2.1tn (4.6% GDP) by 2036
Moody's Aa1 since May 2025 - last AAA lost
FY2027 funding cliff 2026-10-01
Debt limit could re-bind late 2026 under House plan
CBO: long-term trajectory unsustainable
strategic.what_is_priced: Consensus cites the fiscal trajectory as a structural reason for long-term dollar bearishness but, like the market, largely shrugs off the timing. We are aligned: fiscal is a real strategic negative but a poor timing tool, so it reinforces the soft-dollar lean without being the trigger.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical Neutral (no dated fiscal catalyst) versus strategic Bearish (the unsustainable debt trajectory, the lost AAA, and the FY2027 funding cliff weigh on the dollar over 1-6 months, though chronic financeability blunts the timing).

tactical.analysis: The structural picture turned modestly dollar-supportive as the DXY reclaimed the 100 handle on the hawkish Fed. Spot ~100.6 sits above the 50-day (~99.2) and around the 100-day (~99.5) but still below the 200-day (~101.5), so the MA configuration is tangled-turning-up rather than cleanly bullish - a break above ~101.5 would trigger trend-following buying. Key resistance is 100.7 (the June high) then 102.0; support is 99.0, then 98.0 (the 15-June haven-unwind low) and 97.0 (the 2026 four-year low). On volatility, EUR/USD 1M implied vol is ~6.0% (up from 2026 lows ~5.4%) with the IV percentile around the 35th - options remain comparatively cheap. The 25-delta 1M risk reversal favours EUR puts / USD calls (~-0.35 vols), i.e. the options market is paying a modest premium to hedge dollar upside, a mildly bullish structural tell. Realised vol has tracked just below implied, so there is a small event premium rather than under-priced realised moves. The net structural read is mildly dollar-positive: the 100 reclaim plus a downside-skewed EUR/USD risk reversal lean the same way as the fundamental tactical call.
key_data_points: DXY 50d ~99.2, 100d ~99.5, 200d ~101.5 - tangled, turning up
EUR/USD 1M IV ~6.0%, 3M ~6.5%
IV percentile ~35th (1Y) - options cheap
25d 1M risk reversal ~-0.35 vols (USD calls bid)
RV ~5.6% just below IV - small event premium
tactical.what_is_priced: The options market is paying a small premium for dollar upside (risk reversal skewed to USD calls), so the structural tell agrees with the bullish tactical fundamentals. With IV cheap, a directional dollar view is best expressed with long optionality rather than spot, since the market is not over-charging for it.

strategic.analysis: Strategically the structure is Neutral, consistent with a counter-trend recovery inside a 2026 downtrend. The DXY remains below its 200-day MA and within a broad 97-104 range that has contained price all year, so there is no established strategic trend to ride. The 2026 high-water marks near 104 and the early-year lows near 97 bracket the likely strategic range absent a regime break. Vol term structure is close to flat-to-mild-backwardation around event dates (FOMC, CPI), with no persistent event premium, so the options market is not pricing a large strategic move in either direction. For a 1-6 month view, the structural message is that the dollar is range-bound and lacks trend conviction - the fundamental factors (rich valuation, fading carry, fiscal drag) will have to do the directional work, not technicals. We therefore score structure Neutral strategically, with the caveat that a sustained break above 104 or below 97 would itself become a flow catalyst. Cheap strategic vol leaves long-dated optionality attractive given the fat two-sided tails.
key_data_points: DXY range-bound 97-104 through 2026
Spot below 200d MA (~101.5) - no strategic uptrend
Strategic resistance 104; support 97
Vol term structure flat-to-mild backwardation, no persistent premium
IV percentile ~35th - low strategic vol
Break >104 or <97 would be a trend-following flow trigger
strategic.what_is_priced: The vol surface prices a range-bound dollar with no large strategic move embedded - consistent with consensus's modest forecasts. Our edge is recognising that the cheap strategic vol under-prices the convexity around the two regime-defining binaries (Iran-deal durability and Fed-credibility), making long-dated optionality attractive.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical Bullish (the 100-handle reclaim and a USD-call-skewed risk reversal align with the hawkish-Fed fundamentals) versus strategic Neutral (the DXY remains range-bound below its 200-day MA with no trend conviction over 1-6 months).
factors.expression_assessment: With EUR/USD 1M IV around 6.0% near the 35th percentile, options are comparatively cheap, so a directional dollar view carries asymmetric payoff through long optionality rather than spot. The 25-delta risk reversal skewed to USD calls means the market is already paying a small premium for dollar upside, so the cleanest cheap expression of the tactical-bullish view is via EUR/USD puts or USD/JPY calls rather than chasing spot. Because realised vol sits just below implied (a small event premium), there is no strong case to sell vol; and given the fat two-sided strategic tails (Iran durability, Fed credibility), holding long-dated optionality is preferable to a static spot position over the 1-6 month horizon.

tactical.analysis: Analysed from the supply side, US dollar funding conditions are stable and broadly neutral tactically. SOFR printed 3.69% on 15 June, easing to 3.63% on 16 June, sitting right around the IORB (3.65%) - no funding dislocation. The Fed ended QT in December 2025 and has pivoted to maintaining ample reserves, with the balance sheet around $6.7tn and reaffirmed in the June statement. With QT over, the prior mechanical drain on global dollar liquidity has stopped, which is a mild easing of the dollar-supply tightness that had supported the dollar through 2025 - marginally dollar-negative but not acute. Cross-currency basis (3M EUR/USD and JPY/USD) is structurally negative but no fresh June stress print was available; with risk-on conditions and no funding event in the window, basis is assumed near its normal modest-negative range rather than stressed. There is no swap-line usage. The net tactical read is Neutral: funding is calm, the end of QT slightly eases dollar scarcity, and there is no dislocation pushing the dollar toward the strong left of the smile.
key_data_points: QT ended December 2025
Fed balance sheet ~$6.7tn (10 June)
Ample reserves reaffirmed in June statement
No swap-line usage; no funding dislocation
3M xccy basis structurally negative, no fresh stress print
tactical.what_is_priced: The market prices calm dollar funding - no scarcity premium is embedded. The edge is recognising that the end of QT has quietly removed a dollar-supportive liquidity drain, a marginally dollar-negative shift that the market has not focused on amid the louder Fed-rate story.

strategic.analysis: Strategically, dollar liquidity is a balanced factor with weight 0.12. The end of QT and the Fed's commitment to a large, slowly-growing balance sheet mean global dollar supply is no longer being actively drained, which over 1-6 months removes a structural dollar-support and tilts mildly toward the easy-funding bottom of the smile (dollar-soft). Heavy Treasury-bill issuance around the FY2027 funding dynamics and quarter-ends could intermittently tighten money-market liquidity and push the basis more negative (dollar-supportive in those episodes), so the strategic path is two-sided. Reserve balances remain ample but the Fed is watchful for the minimum-comfortable level; any 2025-style repo stress would be acutely dollar-positive via funding scarcity, but that is a tail, not a base case. With QT done and no swap-line stress, the central strategic case is benign, abundant dollar liquidity (mildly dollar-negative) punctuated by issuance-driven tightening episodes. The factor nets to Neutral. Where the dollar sits on the funding-driven smile depends on whether liquidity stays abundant (soft) or an issuance/repo squeeze hits (strong).
key_data_points: QT ended Dec 2025; balance sheet ~$6.7tn, slowly growing
SOFR ~3.63-3.69%, at IORB - calm funding
Heavy T-bill issuance can tighten money markets episodically
Reserves ample, Fed watching minimum level
2025 repo stress is a dollar-positive tail, not base case
No swap-line usage; basis not stressed
strategic.what_is_priced: Consensus prices benign, abundant dollar funding as the base case, consistent with a soft dollar. We agree, while flagging that the market under-prices the convex dollar-positive tail of an issuance- or repo-driven funding squeeze - a low-probability but high-impact left-side-of-smile scenario.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both horizons are Neutral; the end of QT is the common thread (mildly dollar-negative by removing a liquidity drain), with confidence marginally higher tactically because near-term SOFR/basis prints are concrete while the strategic path adds two-sided issuance and repo-squeeze risks.

weighted_score.interpretation: A moderately bullish tactical reading, driven almost entirely by the heavyweight rate and inflation factors (combined +0.295 contribution) reflecting the hawkish Fed repricing, partially offset by the risk and event factors as the Iran de-escalation drains the haven bid.
weighted_score.mapping_to_conviction: A net score of +0.28 with offsetting factors maps to conviction 3 (moderate) - directional but not high-conviction given the two-sided risk channel.

tactical.rationale: The tactical dollar view is Bullish with moderate conviction, and the weighted score of +0.28 is dominated by the two largest-weight factors. Policy & Rates (0.24) and Inflation & Growth (0.18) together contribute +0.295, the entirety of the bullish tilt, because the 16-17 June FOMC recast the 2026 path from cuts to a likely hike and the energy-led CPI surge to 4.2% justifies it. Guidance reinforces this: Warsh's hawkish, forward-guidance-free debut is an immediate dollar-positive. These dollar-positive factors conflict with the risk and event factors: the just-signed Iran ceasefire collapsed oil and the VIX, draining the safe-haven bid and pulling the dollar toward the weak bottom of the smile. The path of least resistance is a modestly higher dollar in the near term, as the rate channel out-weighs the risk channel and momentum/positioning lean long. What is priced: the market has moved about two-thirds of the way to a 2026 hike, so the easy repricing is done; our edge is that sticky core PCE (3.3%) keeps the Fed hawkish through the July data even as oil falls. Key dated catalysts are the 25 June core PCE, the 2 July NFP, the 14 July CPI and the 28-29 July FOMC. The conviction driver is the alignment of rates, inflation, guidance and structure all pointing dollar-up; the conviction cap is the live risk that a single soft inflation print reverses the front-end repricing and that the haven-unwind continues. The view is tradeable but best expressed where carry and momentum align - short EUR/USD or long USD/JPY - rather than via the index, and with cheap optionality given low IV.

g10_ranking.commentary: The dollar sits near the top of the tactical pack on its hawkish-Fed front-end repricing and top-tier policy rate, but the genuinely top-ranked currencies are the high-yielders with hawkish biases and commodity/positioning support (AUD at 4.35% with a tightening bias, NOK at 4.25%). The dollar out-ranks the low-yielders (EUR, JPY, CHF) decisively on carry and momentum.

base_case.outcome: DXY grinds higher toward 101.5-102.0; EUR/USD drifts to 1.150-1.155, USD/JPY toward 161
conditions: July CPI/core PCE stay above 3% core
Iran ceasefire holds, no haven snap-back
base_case.path: A steady grind rather than a spike, as positioning is already long.

upside_case.outcome: DXY breaks above 102 toward 104 on a hawkish surprise or an Iran ceasefire breakdown that restores the haven bid
conditions: Ceasefire cracks, oil and VIX spike

downside_case.outcome: DXY falls back below 99 toward 98 as a soft CPI/PCE print reverses the front-end repricing and risk-on persists
conditions: Core PCE/CPI surprises lower on cheaper oil
Fed-speak walks back the hike signal

tactical.path_expectation: We expect a grind-higher rather than a spike over 2-4 weeks. The dollar should hold the 100 handle and probe 101.5-102.0 into and through the late-June/early-July data cluster, with the 25 June core PCE and 2 July NFP the first tests. Volatility should stay contained (IV ~6%) unless the Iran ceasefire cracks or an inflation print surprises sharply. Expect shallow pullbacks toward 99.5-100.0 to be bought while the hawkish-Fed narrative is intact. The pattern is a low-conviction uptrend that is vulnerable to a sharp reversal on a single soft data point, so position sizing should reflect the two-sided risk.

increase_to_5_if: The Fed actually hikes in July
Core PCE re-accelerates above 3.5% AND the ceasefire breaks down restoring the haven bid
increase_to_4_if: Core PCE/CPI confirm above 3% AND NFP stays firm
decrease_to_3_if: Data are mixed and DXY ranges 99-101 (current)
Fed-speak neither confirms nor walks back the hike signal
decrease_to_2_if: One core inflation print surprises clearly lower
decrease_to_1_if: Core PCE drops below 2.8% AND the Fed pivots dovish

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Spot risk/reward is slightly unfavourable, but the optionality-adjusted distribution skews in the dollar's favour because the haven-tail (ceasefire breakdown) is convex and cheaply priced at VIX 16.

weighted_score.interpretation: A mildly bearish-leaning Neutral. With the rate factor neutralised strategically (synchronized peer tightening compresses the dollar's relative advantage), the score is carried by the two structural negatives - External Balance (weight 0.16) at -0.099 and Fiscal at -0.041 - against no offsetting bullish factor.
weighted_score.mapping_to_conviction: A net score of -0.14 sits in the Neutral band (-0.20 to +0.20) but with a clear bearish tilt, mapping to conviction 3 - a low-conviction lean toward dollar softness, not a directional bearish call.

strategic.rationale: The strategic dollar view is Neutral with a bearish tilt and moderate conviction; the -0.14 weighted score reflects the disappearance of the rate tailwind once the horizon lengthens. At strategic weights, Policy & Rates (0.24) neutralises because the energy shock triggered a synchronized global tightening: the ECB hiked to 2.25%, the BoJ to 1.00%, Norges sits at 4.25%, so even one Fed hike does not widen the dollar's relative carry; it already runs negative carry versus AUD and NOK. With rates neutralised, the second-highest strategic weight, External Balance (0.16), dominates, and it is dollar-bearish: a twin deficit (~3.6% CA + ~5.8% fiscal), a rich REER (107, 78th percentile), and the slow erosion of the USD reserve share all weigh. Fiscal reinforces this. The factors conflict in that Guidance is a wild card: a credible hawkish Warsh Fed would support the dollar, but the Fed-independence tail (politicised easing) is an asymmetric bearish risk. What is priced versus what we expect: consensus is firmly USD-bearish (EUR/USD 1.20-1.25), and we are only mildly so; our edge is that we expect the near-term hawkish-Fed support to keep the dollar firmer than consensus into Q3 before the structural negatives reassert. The path of least resistance over 1-6 months is a range-bound-to-softer dollar as cheaper oil cools inflation, the Fed's hike rationale fades, and the 2027-cut narrative returns. Key dated catalysts: the 28-29 July, 15-16 September (SEP) and 27-28 October FOMC meetings, the mid-August Iran nuclear deadline, the 1 October funding cliff, and the 3 November midterms. The conviction driver is the structural valuation/twin-deficit case; the conviction cap is the genuine possibility that the Fed out-hikes the field and US exceptionalism persists. The view is more a relative-value lean (favour the high-yield hawks over the dollar) than a clean directional short.

g10_ranking.commentary: Strategically the dollar slips to mid-table as rich valuation and twin deficits offset its rate level, while the commodity/hawkish high-yielders (AUD, NOK) and the appreciation-policy SGD rank above it. The dollar still beats the structurally low-yielding funders (JPY, CHF) and the weaker cyclicals (CAD, GBP).

base_case.outcome: DXY ranges 97-101, drifting toward the lower half; EUR/USD recovers toward 1.18-1.20
conditions: Iran ceasefire broadly holds, oil stays sub-$85
Fed delivers at most one hike then signals patience
base_case.path: A high-plateau-then-fade: firm into Q3, softer into year-end as disinflation removes the hike rationale.

upside_case.outcome: DXY breaks above 104 if US exceptionalism persists and the Fed hikes while peers stall, or a risk-off event restores the haven bid
conditions: Global growth scare or ceasefire breakdown

downside_case.outcome: DXY breaks below 97 toward 95 as the Fed reverts to the 2027-cut path and twin-deficit/valuation gravity reasserts
conditions: Fed-independence concerns or a politicised cut hit credibility

strategic.path_expectation: We expect a range-bound-to-softer dollar that holds firmer than consensus into Q3 then drifts lower into year-end. The near-term hawkish-Fed support should keep DXY in the upper half of the 97-104 range through July-August, with the September SEP a key inflection: if oil-driven disinflation is visible by then, the dot plot likely walks back the hike and the dollar softens toward 97-99 into Q4. The pattern is a high-plateau-then-fade rather than a trend, with the mid-August Iran deadline and the November midterms as the main volatility nodes. Expect EUR/USD to grind from 1.16 toward 1.18-1.20 as the dominant strategic expression. Pullbacks in EUR/USD toward 1.15 should be accumulation zones for the structural dollar-soft view.

increase_to_5_if: Clear disinflation toward 2.5% core PCE AND the Fed signals 2027 cuts
A politicised Fed cut undermines credibility and DXY breaks below 97
increase_to_4_if: Oil stays sub-$80 and headline CPI falls below 3% by September
decrease_to_3_if: The inflation/rate path stays ambiguous and DXY ranges 99-102 (current)
No clear disinflation or re-acceleration signal
decrease_to_2_if: US exceptionalism widens and the Fed delivers a clear hike
decrease_to_1_if: The Fed embarks on a hiking cycle
A sustained risk-off restores the structural haven bid and DXY breaks above 104

risk_reward.downside_case_loss: EUR/USD -160 pips to ~1.145, or a tail break to 1.12 in a haven event
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Modestly favourable for the strategic dollar-soft lean given rich valuation caps dollar upside, but the haven/exceptionalism tail keeps it from being high-conviction; best sized as a relative-value lean rather than an outright dollar short.

consensus_divergence.consensus_view: Banks set forecasts earlier in 2026 that are broadly USD-bearish - EUR/USD 1.20-1.25 year-end (Goldman ~1.25, MUFG ~1.24, ING/JPM ~1.20-1.22), DXY drifting to the mid-to-high 90s, premised on Fed cuts, a post-peak dollar world, and twin-deficit/reserve-diversification gravity.
consensus_divergence.our_view: We are tactically more dollar-bullish than consensus and strategically only mildly bearish - less bearish than the Street near-term. The hawkish June Fed turn (a likely hike, not cuts) is a regime change much of the consensus forecast predates, and we expect it to keep the dollar firmer than the 1.20-1.25 path through Q3.
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: Tactically material (EUR/USD 1.15-1.16 vs a consensus glide toward 1.20+); strategically converging by year-end but on a higher path (EUR/USD 1.16-1.18 vs 1.20-1.25).
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: The market under-weights how decisively the energy shock and the Warsh dot plot flipped the Fed's reaction function - most bearish forecasts assume cuts that are no longer the base case. We also weight the synchronized-peer-tightening offset: it caps the dollar's strategic upside (agreeing with consensus) but equally undercuts the assumption that the dollar's rate advantage simply collapses.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: Being long the dollar (or short EUR/USD) into Q3 against a Street positioned for 1.20+ captures a ~300-500 pip move if EUR/USD holds 1.15-1.16 while consensus targets are missed.
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: If oil-driven disinflation arrives fast and the Fed reverts to cuts, the consensus 1.20-1.25 path materialises quickly and our firmer-dollar near-term call is stopped out as the structural bears are vindicated early.

cleanest_bullish_expression.rationale: The rate channel is the widest in the G10 (UST-JGB 10Y +274bps, USD carry +262bps), momentum and trend align, and the BoJ at just 1.00% cannot close the gap quickly - the hawkish-Fed dollar expresses most cleanly here, with the caveat that MoF intervention near 161 caps the upside.

cleanest_bearish_expression.rationale: Long AUD: the RBA at 4.35% with a tightening bias gives AUD negative-carry-for-USD (-72bps), commodity and risk-on tailwinds, and the strongest 1Y momentum (+9.1%) - the cleanest way to be short the dollar against a hawkish high-yielder rather than a low-yield funder.

relative_value_pairs.thesis: Expresses dollar-neutrality while harvesting the carry and momentum spread between the hawkish high-yielder (AUD) and the rate-disadvantaged funder (JPY), insulating against a broad dollar move.
Norges at 4.25% with a hawkish bias and a current-account surplus (+12.4% GDP) offers a high-quality short-USD expression less exposed to risk-off than AUD.

cross_horizon_consistency.summary: Tactical Bullish, strategic Neutral (bearish tilt): the dollar is supported now by a hawkish Fed repricing but the support fades as the horizon lengthens and structural negatives dominate.
cross_horizon_consistency.divergence_explanation: The tactical bull case rests on the heavy-weight rate and inflation factors that turned dollar-positive at the June FOMC; strategically those factors neutralise (synchronized peer tightening, oil-driven disinflation) and the high-strategic-weight External Balance and Fiscal factors take over, both dollar-bearish.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: Roughly the September SEP (15-16 September), when the durability of the inflation spike and the Fed's hike intent will be clarified.
cross_horizon_consistency.catalyst_for_transition: Visible oil-driven disinflation in the July-August CPI/PCE prints removing the Fed-hike rationale.
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Run the tactical dollar long with a tight leash and a defined exit around the late-July/September data, then rotate toward the strategic dollar-soft/relative-value stance as the hike narrative fades.

1m.annualised_pct: +1.9% (EUR earns forward points; USD at a forward discount)

edge.1m: ~-100 pips (forward implies 1.1632, we expect ~1.153 - more dollar-bullish than the forward)
edge.3m: ~-90 pips (we expect a firmer dollar than the forward into Q3)
edge.6m: ~+120 pips (we expect EUR/USD above the forward by year-end)

forward_vs_spot.interpretation: The interest-rate differential puts EUR/USD at a forward premium (the dollar is expected to weaken in the forwards purely from carry), but our view diverges by horizon: near-term we expect the dollar firmer than the forwards imply (negative edge to being long EUR/USD at 1M-3M), while at 6M we expect EUR/USD above the forward as the strategic dollar-soft case plays out. The forward curve and our path cross around the 3-month mark - consistent with the tactical-bullish/strategic-neutral split and the September inflection.

key_catalysts.event: US Core PCE (May) & Q1 GDP third estimate
key_catalysts.rationale: Core PCE is the Fed's preferred gauge; a sticky print validates the hawkish dot plot and supports the front-end repricing. A soft print would be the first crack in the hike thesis.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially - market expects core PCE near 3.3%; a beat reprices hike odds higher

key_catalysts.rationale: NFP is the highest-impact US release alongside CPI; a firm print (after +172k in May) keeps the Fed hawkish, a soft one reopens the cut narrative.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Not priced - consensus forms closer to the date

key_catalysts.rationale: With headline at 4.2%, the June print tests whether cheaper oil is already cooling inflation; a still-hot core keeps the Fed hawkish.
After June's hike to 2.25%, further ECB hawkishness narrows the USD-EUR rate gap and supports EUR/USD; a dovish hold supports the dollar.
The first meeting where the June dot-plot hike could materialise; even a hawkish hold sustains the dollar, an actual hike would push DXY toward 104.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially - ~66% odds of >=1 hike in 2026 across the year, not specifically July

key_catalysts.event: US Core PCE (June) & Q2 GDP advance
key_catalysts.rationale: Q2 GDP (GDPNow ~3%) tests US exceptionalism; core PCE tests inflation persistence - together they shape the September hike decision.
With the BoJ at 1.00% and USD/JPY near 160/intervention territory, any hawkish step or intervention signal would squeeze the crowded short-JPY/long-USD trade.
By July, cheaper post-ceasefire oil should be dragging headline CPI down; a clear deceleration undercuts the Fed-hike rationale and the dollar.
The 60-day technical window from the 14 June ceasefire expires ~mid-August; a durable deal cements risk-on (dollar-soft), a breakdown restores the haven bid (dollar-strong).
key_catalysts.priced_in: Market prices a durable outcome - breakdown tail under-priced
key_catalysts.bilateral_relevance: All USD pairs (haven channel), oil-linked CAD/NOK

key_catalysts.rationale: A fresh dot plot; whether the hike survives depends on whether oil-driven disinflation has appeared. This is the meeting most likely to flip the dollar from firm to soft.
FY2026 funding lapses 30 September; a shutdown or messy CR injects Treasury-market and political volatility into the pre-midterm window, a mild dollar negative.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Not priced - markets ignore until the deadline nears

key_catalysts.rationale: Late-cycle meeting where the market has speculated a hike could land; by now the oil/inflation path and the durability of US exceptionalism will be clearer.
All 435 House and 35 Senate seats; the outcome shapes the fiscal and trade trajectory and any Fed-pressure dynamics into 2027 - a binary political-risk node.
Year-end meeting and final 2026 dot plot; sets the 2027 path that consensus expects to be dollar-bearish (cuts).

invalidators.condition: The Fed delivers a 25bp hike at the July or September FOMC
invalidators.mechanism: An active hiking cycle would re-establish a durable dollar rate advantage even against tightening peers.

invalidators.condition: Core PCE falls below 2.8% YoY on cheaper oil
invalidators.mechanism: The principal justification for the hawkish dot plot evaporates and the front end reprices toward cuts.

invalidators.condition: The Iran ceasefire breaks down and Brent re-spikes above $95
invalidators.mechanism: The haven bid and the energy-inflation premium both return, lifting the dollar via the left side of the smile.

invalidators.condition: A politicised Fed rate cut occurs under perceived political pressure
invalidators.mechanism: The dollar's institutional-credibility premium erodes as Fed independence is questioned.
invalidators.quantitative_threshold: An unexpected cut not justified by the data

invalidators.mechanism: The dominant expression confirms a dollar uptrend rather than a range, triggering trend-following flows.

invalidators.condition: US ISM Manufacturing and Services both fall below 50
invalidators.mechanism: The exceptionalism pillar collapses, removing the relative-growth support for the dollar.

invalidators.condition: A 2025-style repo/funding squeeze pushes SOFR materially above IORB
invalidators.mechanism: Funding scarcity pushes the dollar to the strong left of the smile via the liquidity channel.

signposts.threshold_invalidates: Re-spikes above $95 (ceasefire breakdown, haven bid returns)
signposts.bilateral_importance: USD/CAD, USD/NOK; haven channel for all

signposts.threshold_invalidates: Falls below 3.90% (front end reprices to cuts)

signposts.threshold_confirms: Stays below 18 (risk-on, dollar at bottom of smile - strategic-soft)
signposts.threshold_invalidates: Spikes above 25 (risk-off, dollar haven bid returns - bullish USD)

factors_changed.reason: The June FOMC flipped the dot plot from cuts to a likely hike under new Chair Warsh.
The energy-led CPI surge to 4.2% turned the near-term data hawkish.
The Iran ceasefire reduced acute risk but left a two-sided strategic picture.
Warsh's hawkish, anti-inflation debut reversed the prior dovish-guidance read.
QT ended and funding is calm, removing the prior tightening read.

signposts_triggered.outcome: Brent collapsed below $80 on the Iran ceasefire
signposts_triggered.implication: The energy-inflation premium that had been building reversed - disinflationary into H2

signposts_triggered.outcome: Moved hawkish (3.4%->3.8% median end-2026) rather than dovish
signposts_triggered.implication: Opposite of the prior report's easing expectation - dollar-supportive regime change

conviction_change.explanation: Tactical stance flipped Bearish->Bullish (conviction 2->3) on the hawkish Fed; strategic stance softened Bearish->Neutral-with-bearish-tilt at unchanged conviction 3.

new_catalysts: US-Iran ceasefire and its mid-August nuclear deadline
Warsh-led Fed's hawkish turn / June dot plot

seasonality.pattern: No strongly documented USD-specific seasonal pattern reliably within the horizon; the general year-end portfolio-rebalancing effect sits at the far edge of the strategic window.
seasonality.historical_magnitude: Modest and inconsistent for the broad dollar
seasonality.current_alignment: Not a primary driver of this report's stance; we do not lean on seasonality.

assumptions: USD positioning is derived from the aggregate of G10 CFTC net positions (a net-short-G10 position implies net-long-USD), since no single direct USD futures contract exists; the percentile is an estimate against the 3-year aggregate range.
Core PCE (3.3%, April) is used as the Fed's preferred core measure; the May print (due 25 June) was not yet available at valuation, so the most recent real value is cited and flagged.

DXY level (~100.6), EUR/USD implied vol, and cross-currency basis are bridged from web sources where the cache was silent or stale; the cache oil/risk prints (2026-06-08) were superseded by live post-ceasefire data.

Macro comparison figures for the euro area (GDP, CPI), Japan (CPI), and Singapore are bridged from web/Eurostat/SingStat-type sources as the OECD cache had gaps; these carry lower confidence than the FRED/OECD-sourced US figures.
Forward points for EUR/USD are approximated from the interest-rate differential; outright forwards are indicative.
The 2Y yield (4.20%) and 2s10s (+29bps) reflect the post-FOMC repricing and are slightly higher/flatter than the early-month FRED cache prints (4.07%/+38bps), which are noted as stale.
